Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd just kind of fit things in whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Most of my studying happened in random bursts - maybe I'd get into it for a few hours one day, then not touch anything for several days. I learn better by doing stuff with my hands anyway, so sitting down to study in a structured way never really clicked for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than anything else. If something was due, I'd cram and get it done. Otherwise, I'd probably be at practice or hanging out instead of studying. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks barely anything - it really just depended on what else was going on in my life.
Q3: Usually a deadline coming up or getting an email notification about something. I wasn't one of those people who just logged in to browse around or stay on top of things. If there wasn't a specific reason to be there, I probably wasn't going to bother. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. It never became automatic for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, but then I'd drift away again. The LMS always felt like a chore rather than something that naturally fit into my day. I'd rather be doing something more hands-on than clicking through modules.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could actually see things being demonstrated rather than just reading about them. I watched maybe 45 minutes worth on average, which helped when I needed to understand concepts quickly. Discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that made more sense to me than the official materials.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary stuff. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably didn't touch it. The discussion forums I only participated in when required - I posted about 12 times total. A lot of the optional resources just felt like busywork that wouldn't actually help me perform better.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection between the activity and my grade, it felt worth doing. Also, anything that was more interactive or practical grabbed my attention better than passive reading. I needed to feel like I was actually accomplishing something concrete, not just going through motions. If it was boring and seemed pointless, I'd check out mentally.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me unless I was really struggling with something specific. Required activities I'd do because I had to, but even then I'd often wait until the last possible moment. Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, especially when I was already bored with the course material.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download stuff and work through it on my own time without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations that made more sense to me - sometimes random people on the internet explain things better than official course materials. I'd work through problems on paper too, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be studying from downloaded PDFs or working through practice problems on paper while the LMS showed nothing. I'd also study with friends from class in person, talking through concepts and quizzing each other. That kind of collaborative, hands-on learning was actually more effective for me but left zero digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or document once, then reference it multiple times offline. My exam score was pretty solid at 88%, and a lot of that prep happened completely offline. I'd print things out sometimes too because staring at screens gets old, and I focus better with physical materials I can mark up.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough when everything piled up at once. I had extracurriculars demanding my time, and the course content started feeling repetitive and boring. My attendance dropped because I just couldn't make myself care enough to show up when I could learn the same stuff faster on my own. The boredom really killed my motivation to engage.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired from other stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars definitely ate into my study time. When I was fatigued, I'd just skim through materials without really absorbing anything. The anxiety about falling behind sometimes made it worse because I'd avoid logging in altogether.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times I'd be logged in but just going through the motions. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, or I'd click through readings without actually processing them. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really engaging with what people were saying. My mind would wander because the material just didn't grab me.
Q15: I procrastinated on assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. I think it was a mix of boredom and feeling like I could pull it off last minute anyway. The CBT sessions I had helped me understand this pattern better, but breaking it was another story. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd find another excuse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do stuff. I'd see something was due in two days and suddenly become super productive. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined in between. It's not a great system, but it worked well enough for me to still get a distinction somehow.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but honestly not as much as it probably should have. If I got good feedback, I'd feel validated but not necessarily motivated to do more. Bad feedback would stress me out but I'd usually just move on rather than deeply engaging with how to improve. The delay between submission and feedback made it feel disconnected from my learning.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid in ways that didn't suit how I learn. I'm a kinesthetic learner - I need to do things, not just read about them. When the course had more practical components, I was more engaged. The heavy reliance on online modules and readings made it easy for me to drift away and lose interest.
Q19: The lack of hands-on activities made engagement harder for me. I would've benefited from more interactive simulations or practical exercises. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic, which made me less interested in participating beyond the minimum. Having everything online also made it too easy to just not show up.
Q20: I think those students might be like me - logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos in the background, but not actually producing work until absolutely necessary. You can rack up a lot of activity just by being logged in and browsing without actually completing assignments. It's easy to confuse presence with productivity.
Q21: That's literally me. My LMS engagement was low, but I still pulled off a distinction. I'm good at exams when I actually sit down and study, and I did a lot of learning offline that doesn't show up in the data. Some people just don't need to be constantly online to learn effectively - we find other ways that work better for us.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They capture activity but not understanding or genuine engagement.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and being mentally present when you're studying. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos. Real engagement is when the material interests you enough that you think about it even when you're not studying. I struggled with that this semester because a lot of it just felt boring.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I learned through hands-on practice outside the system, or how my extracurriculars actually helped me develop skills relevant to the course. It wouldn't capture the stress and boredom I was dealing with, or how the CBT sessions affected my approach to studying. The numbers tell one story, but my actual experience was way more complicated.
